---
title: "Practical Machine Learning - Prediction Assignment Writeup"
output: html_document
self_contained: FALSE
keep_md: TRUE
---


```{r cache=TRUE}
library(caret)
library(randomForest)
library(rattle)
#Function for converting data types of multiple columns
#From http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11261399/function-for-converting-dataframe-column-type
convert.magic <- function(obj, type, cols) {
    FUN1 <- switch(type, character = as.character, numeric = as.numeric, factor = as.factor)
    obj[,cols] <- lapply(obj[,cols], FUN1) 
    as.data.frame(obj)
}
#Download data. This may take a little while (about 35 seconds on my laptop.)
if (!file.exists("pml-training.csv")) {
    download.file(url="https://d396qusza40orc.cloudfront.net/predmachlearn/pml-training.csv",
                  destfile="pml-training.csv")
}
if (!file.exists("pml-testing.csv")) {
    download.file(url="https://d396qusza40orc.cloudfront.net/predmachlearn/pml-testing.csv",
                  destfile="pml-testing.csv")
}
#round(prop.table(table(trainingRaw$user_name, trainingRaw$classe), 1), 2)
#Load data and convert all blanks, spaces, string NAs and #DIV/0! to a true NA values
trainingRaw <- read.csv("pml-training.csv", header=TRUE, na.strings = c("", " ", "NA", "#DIV/0!"))
testingRaw <- read.csv("pml-testing.csv", header=TRUE, na.strings = c("", " ","NA", "#DIV/0!"))
# Remove columns with more than 500 NA values
trainingClean <- trainingRaw[!colSums(is.na(trainingRaw)) > 500]
# Filter rows to only those that have new_window == "no"
trainingClean <- subset(trainingClean, subset = (new_window == "no"))
# Remove metadata columns
trainingClean <- trainingClean[,-(1:7),drop=FALSE]
# Change all data types to numeric (with the exception of classe)
trainingClean <- convert.magic(trainingClean,type = "numeric",c(1:52))
# Split clean raw training set into training and testing sets based on classe
trainIndex = createDataPartition(trainingClean$classe, p = 0.70, list=FALSE)
trainingClean.training = trainingClean[trainIndex,]
trainingClean.testing = trainingClean[-trainIndex,]
# Set a seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123654)
# Let's try a decision tree algorithm to see how well it does
modFit.rpart <- train(classe ~., method = "rpart", data = trainingClean.training)
# Show decision tree on a fancy format
fancyRpartPlot(modFit.rpart$finalModel)
# Use the decisision tree model to predict classe for the clean raw testing set
pred.rpart <- as.vector(predict(modFit.rpart, trainingClean.testing))
# Must be pretty bad as it never predicts any classe = "D"
# Let's check accuraccy for the decision tree model
pred.rpart.correct <- sum(pred.rpart==trainingClean.testing$classe)
pred.rpart.accuracy <- pred.rpart.correct / nrow(trainingClean.testing)
# About 49.5% (i.e. pretty much the same as a coin flip)

# Let's try a random forest algorithm for our first prediction model
modFit.rf <- randomForest(classe ~ ., data = trainingClean.training)
# Use the randomForest model to predict classe for the clean raw testing set
pred.rf <- as.vector(predict(modFit.rf, trainingClean.testing))
# Get the confusion matrix to see how accurate is our prediction
confusionMatrix(pred.rf, trainingClean.testing$classe)[c("table","overall")]

# Let's then try a boosting algorithm to see if it does better than the random forest
modFit.gbm <- train(classe ~ ., method = "gbm", data = trainingClean.training, verbose = FALSE)
# Use the boosting model to predict classe for the clean raw testing set
pred.gbm <- as.vector(predict(modFit.rf, trainingClean.testing))
# Get the confusion matrix to see how accurate is our prediction
confusionMatrix(pred.gbm, trainingClean.testing$classe)[c("table","overall")]

#qplot(pred.gbm, classe, data = trainingClean.testing, col = classe, position=position_jitter(w=0.05,h=0.05))
#he num_window and "X" variables in particular are not appropriate to include. The test subjects seem to run through their different exercise techniques sequentially. If you graph num_window and vs. Class you will see the cyclic correlation. So, if you use num_window to predict, you may "overfit", However, I don't mean that your predictions on the test data will suffer (they may actually get better) - but you will not be predicting the "classe" using the measurements of the device - you will be predicting based on when and in what sequence the exercise was performed
answers <- as.vector(predict(modFit.rf, testingRaw))
```
